results.title: Amsterdam | DC Byte
results.text:
Amsterdam | DC Byte

# Amsterdam

Supply: 1,849 MW

Total Supply is the sum of four supply categories: Live, Under Construction, Committed, and Early Stage.

Facilities: 90

## Overview

Amsterdam is one of the most established data centre markets in the world thanks to its geographic position as a gateway to Europe and its world-class internet exchanges, particularly AMS-IX. Major growth was seen between 2010 and 2018 and was then curtailed by a moratorium in 2019. Since the moratorium was lifted in 2020, development has picked up but the rate of activity has reduced with other markets offering strong alternatives.

As with other markets, power availability is a particular challenge for the Amsterdam market, with demand for electricity outstripping supply and leading to ‘traffic jams’ on the power grid. For data centres, power supply is affecting the market with many power distributions firms saying that they have no power available until 2030, thus limiting development.

Significantly, since the start of 2024, an amended decree has meant that no new hyperscale data centres (<10 hectares/70+ MW) are allowed to be constructed in the Netherlands, except for a few locations in the municipalities of Het Hogeland (e.g. Eemshaven) and Hollands Kroon (e.g. Agriport). Further regulation is planned for the Municipality of Amsterdam with a recent draft umbrella zoning plan suggesting that 350 MVA is the new maximum connection capacity within the municipality until 2030. Additionally,

results.title: Data Centres in Amsterdam | DC Byte
results.text:
Data Centres in Amsterdam | DC Byte

# Data Centres in Amsterdam

by dinah.anggreani@dcbyte.com on March 21, 2024 in Market Infographics

March 21, 2024

Amsterdam, capital city of the Netherlands, is one of the leading data centre hubs across Europe.

These hubs are known as the FLAP-D (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin).

Since 2010, Amsterdam and North of the Netherlands have added just over 800MW of Live IT Capacity. The encouragement of foreign direct investment, strong connectivity, strong digital infrastructure among other factors have facilitated the growth of the data centre scene in Amsterdam. These have allowed both colocation and self-build operators to have a strong presence in the market. However, new supply in the market has been limited due to regulations and power availability.

Read on to our infographic for a snapshot of the Amsterdam data centre market, covering aspects such as the supply, data centre clusters, market share and more.

<< Back to Market Infographics

results.title: FLAP-D and Beyond: Decoupling Strategy from Capacity in EMEA | DC Byte
results.text:
FLAP-D and Beyond: Decoupling Strategy from Capacity in EMEA | DC Byte

# FLAP-D and Beyond: Decoupling Strategy from Capacity in EMEA

by valentina.sarzhevska@dcbyte.com on February 17, 2026 in Blog, News & Blogs

February 17, 2026

EMEA’s hyperscale narrative remains structurally anchored in the FLAP-D markets (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin). These hubs represent the primary centers of gravity for interconnection density and cloud deployment.

However, the competitive moat is shifting. Market advantage is no longer derived from mere demand adjacency; it is now defined by deliverability under systemic constraint. Power procurement, planning pathways, and site readiness now dictate deployment velocity as much as customer proximity. For sophisticated observers, the primary signal is no longer live capacity, but the committed pipeline relative to operational supply.

“The pivot in EMEA conversations is clear: we are moving from demand mapping to execution viability. The leading indicator is not current capacity, but the formation of the committed pipeline—specifically, how much is pre-let and which delivery models are being utilized to mitigate execution risk.” — Scott Roots, Sales Director, EMEA, DC Byte.

## London and Frankfurt: Convergent Fundamentals, Divergent Conversion Risks

While distinct, London and Frankfurt share a strategic role as the twin pillars of European finance and enterprise. Both anchor deep connectivity ecosystems with durable demand dr

2025 Global Data Centre Index | DC Byte

# 2025 Global Data Centre Index

April 29, 2025

### The Global Index 2025: A Critical Benchmark for the World’s Digital Infrastructure

Demand has never been stronger. But while hyperscalers and investors continue to pour billions into digital infrastructure, the industry is hitting a wall. Supply bottlenecks are threatening to derail future growth.

Our flagship Global Data Centre Index 2025 provides a comprehensive analysis of market supply, demand and development trends across the Americas, APAC and EMEA. This is based on the latest real-world data and analysis from DC Byte’s research team.

### Why This Index Matters: Strategic Insight for Operators, Investors and Planners

This complimentary report equips you with the data and context to:

- Track Global Supply-Demand Dynamics: Discover how demand has outpaced supply growth across all regions, with 2024 take-up up nearly 30% year-on-year.
- Understand Pipeline Bottlenecks: Learn why committed capacity is growing while construction slows. Power and permitting challenges are now defining the pace of development.
- Unpack AI’s Impact on Infrastructure: Explore how hyperscale and AI-led demand is reshaping data centre deal sizes and delivery timelines. Benchmark Regional Growth: Compare growth rates across the Americas, APAC and EMEA from 2019 to 2024. See which markets are driving future expansion.

results.title: Quarterly Report: Q4 2023 | DC Byte
results.text:
Quarterly Report: Q4 2023 | DC Byte

# Q4 2023

by dinah.anggreani@dcbyte.com on April 12, 2024 in Knight Frank Quarterly Reports

## Overview

This quarter’s Knight Frank report focuses on the EMEA region. Market analysis includes both established data centre hubs such as Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin; and emerging markets including Madrid, Sweden, Berlin, Denmark, Milan, and the UAE.

AI requirements and continued adoption of cloud services are driving demand in established markets and secondary markets to record levels. The markets covered in this report demonstrated an increase of approximately 2,953MW in aggregate supply over 2023, with Frankfurt and London seeing 744MW and 499MW respectively. Companies such as Microsoft, META, AWS and Google have all recently realised earnings for the end of 2023, with the principal theme being strong financial performances with AI acting as a major growth driver.

As AI applications become more prevalent across various regions, the demand for data centres to accommodate this surge will significantly increase. AI functions, especially AI training, require exceptional amounts of data storage and processing thus creating demand for larger, more powerful data centres. In line with this, Microsoft have announced that they intend to invest £2.5billion to develop AI infrastructures in the UK with Wales being a potential starting point for the company. London registered 56MW of capacity absorbed over the course of the year. A

results.title: Data centers - statistics & facts
results.text:
Data centers - statistics & facts | Statista

# Data centers - statistics & facts

Data centers lie at the heart of the AI revolution. Housing IT equipment organized into racks, data centers are powering AI, the cloud, and other data-intensive technologies by efficiently processing workloads and storing data securely. Once used to describe small, on-site server rooms for businesses, the term 'data center' now refers to vast, power-intensive facilities operated by global tech giants. Colocation operators such as Equinix and Digital Realty offer data center capacity for rent at shared facilities across the globe, while big tech firms such as Meta and Google operate their own hyperscale facilities to power cloud and AI offerings. With data center demand surging across the globe, governments and regulators are grappling with how to nurture this essential digital infrastructure while addressing sustainability concerns.

## Key markets feel the strain

Major data center operators have typically looked to cluster facilities in established hubs with favorable local infrastructure and proximity to major population centers. The Northern Virginia region is undoubtedly the world’s largest single data center hub, offering the most inventory of any region, while the FLAPD (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin) markets serve as key European hubs. However, with global data center supply projected to grow by more than 20 percent in 2025 alone, the pace and scale of expansion has pr

results.title: Data Centres | CBRE Nederland
results.text:
Data Centres | CBRE Nederland

Hoofdstuk 8

# Data Centres

## European Real Estate Market Outlook Mid-Year Review 2025

Data Centres

##### January 2025 Forecast

## Significant new supply to be added in 2025

Despite a distinct lack of available power, data centre supply across Europe's five largest markets is expected to grow by a record 20% year-on-year in 2025. London and Frankfurt are expected to account for 2.5GW of capacity, or approximately half of the total data centre supply in Europe, by the end of 2025. They are forecast to be 1.3GW and 1.2GW markets, respectively.

### Mid-year review

- Most new supply in Europe (70%) will be delivered to the five largest markets – FLAPD (Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin) this year. Nevertheless, there will be seven secondary markets registering over 100MW of supply. Four years ago, there were no secondary markets of 100MW or more. This demonstrates that data centre investment is increasingly diversified in Europe.
- We forecast that new supply will grow by 17% across Europe year-on-year in 2025, based on our analysis of the 15 top European data centre markets.

##### January 2025 Forecast

## Vacancy rates to hit historic lows

The vacancy rate in Europe is expected to close at 9% in 2025, a new low. The top 15 European data centre markets combined have traditionally seen a double-digit vacancy rate. However, available space is expected to decline for the fourth consecutive year, given strong demand for capacity

results.title: Data centres | CBRE
results.text:
Data centres | CBRE

Chapter 10

# Data centres

## Netherlands Real Estate Market Outlook 2025

By Frank Verwoerd Erik Langens

By Frank Verwoerd Erik Langens

Data centres

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Worldwide, the data centres market has grown significantly in recent years. This is partly due to cloud computing, big data, the (further) increasing digitalisation and the increasing demand for data services such as AI. Together with Frankfurt, London, Paris and Dublin, Amsterdam is one of the five most important colocation markets[14] in the European data centre market. A position owed to a strategic location and a good digital infrastructure. But while demand for data services and capacity in our country continues to increase, growth in the Netherlands is limited. This results in scarcity.

### Trends and developments

- The amount of data is growing exponentially due to developments such as AI and digitization. As a result, the demand for data centre is also increasing. In five years' time, this has led to a significant decrease in vacancy in and around Amsterdam: from 24% to almost 9%. This is particularly low in the data centre sector. Moreover, due to power grid congestion and restrictive regulations, there is hardly any room for expansion. The vacancy rate is therefore only expected to decrease further.
- With a capacity of more than 500 megawatts, Amsterdam is currently the third largest colocation data centre hub in Europe. But while other prime markets,

results.title: Europe Data Centres
results.text:
FIGURES | EUROPE DATA CENTRES | Q4 2024

## Europe Data Centres

#### Nearly 1GW of data centre capacity to be added in Europe in 2025

A lack of availability in FLAPD, growing demand for wholesale capacity from hyperscalers, and data protection/data residency regulation are factors leading to double-digit supply growth of Europe’s secondary markets. Cloud service providers increasingly want to deliver services to local markets from in-country, as opposed to in-region, and this has led to growth of markets beyond FLAPD. Enterprises are looking to outsource their equipment to trusted third parties as well.

#### Seven secondary markets to be larger than 100MW by year end

Note: Figures are representative of top 15 European markets covered by CBRE. Arrows indicate change from same quarter in previous year. 
For take-up, the figure represents a comparison of Q4 2024 versus Q3 2024. 

4,726MW European colocation supply 470MW European colocation availability 341MW European colocation take-up

CBRE expects almost 1GW of new data centre supply to be delivered (937MW) in 2025 as providers aim to keep pace with strong demand. Should our forecast come to pass, total supply will have grown 20% by year end. Most (677MW) of the new supply is expected in the primary markets of Europe.

More space is needed given growing demand. Take-up exceeded the new supply added in Europe last year; it was the fourth such occurrence in the last eight years. Availability fell to a new low. Providers are

results.title: New Investment in Europe’s Data Centre Markets to Hit New Heights in 2025 | CBRE UK
results.text:
New Investment in Europe’s Data Centre Markets to Hit New Heights in 2025 | CBRE UK

Press Release

# New Investment in Europe’s Data Centre Markets to Hit New Heights in 2025

February 13, 2025

View All Press Releases

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

### Media Contact

Charlotte Kenna

Director

- Mobile +44 (0) 7799 717 813
- Email

According to new research from CBRE, there are 937MW of new supply expected in European markets this year. If this data centre supply is delivered, it will set a new record for Europe and would represent an increase of 282MW more than the 655MW of capacity delivered in 2024.

More than half (57%) of this capacity is expected to be delivered in the leading European data centre markets - Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin.

In 2024, facilities of unprecedented size were delivered across Europe for the industry’s largest customers, but demand for colocation data centre space continued to outstrip supply, at 706MW and 655MW respectively. Customer appetite for capacity has grown despite the difficulties data centre providers are having sourcing power and available land for new facilities.

Nevertheless, a greater proportion of supply will be delivered to Europe’s smaller secondary markets than ever before. CBRE expects double-digit supply growth in five of the 10 secondary European markets tracked in 2025. There are seven markets, such as Milan and Madrid, expected to have 100MW of supply or more by ye

results.title: European Data Centres Overview | CBRE
results.text:
European Data Centres Overview | CBRE

Intelligent Investment

# European Data Centres Overview

July 22, 2024 10 Minute Read

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Download

### About This Report

Data centres play an increasingly important role in the world. The purpose-built facilities house computer systems and components, such as telecommunications and storage systems, designed to enable business processes, government services, and social interactions.

Data centres form the foundation of the digital economy, hosting the cloud, and storing our data.

However, the role of data centres is not always apparent since data centres are often located in trading estates or industrial sites formerly occupied by heavy industries.

This report aims to provide a better understanding of the fundamentals of the data centre sector. We detail the growth drivers of the asset class, the size of Europe’s largest colocation data centre markets, the type of investors involved, and key considerations of development and investment in the sector.

## Key Takeaways

>1GW

Live capacity in London, making it Europe’s largest data centre market

85%

Of take-up was recorded in FLAPD* last year *Represents the five largest colocation markets in Europe: Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin

5.8GW

Data centre capacity expected across Europe by the end of 2024

Access the full report to gain insights into this major asset
